sound_type: loud rattle call + unique drum -- sharp staccato burst carrying across open post-fire landscape, dry percussive drum on charred snags, E=5
ecosystem: Burn forest -- recently burned conifer and mixed forest, 1-8 years post-fire. Standing dead trees (snags) colonized by bark beetles. Open, canopy-free landscape. The post-fire environment as acoustic stage.
season: Year-round resident. Does not migrate. Nomadic following fire events -- may move dozens or hundreds of kilometers to reach recently burned areas.

coordinates.description: Reference for montane burn habitat in the North Cascades. Black-backed Woodpeckers found in recently burned conifer forest throughout the boreal and montane West.

taxonomy_note.description: NINTH CONSECUTIVE PICIDAE (107-115) -- ALL-TIME FAMILY RECORD surpassing the Sciuridae octet. STREAK CLOSES at degree 115. Second fire-specialist after Lewis's Woodpecker (112). Three-toed adaptation shared with Three-toed Woodpecker (114). Obligate fire-follower. Conservation paradox: needs fire, but fire suppression reduces habitat.
taxonomy_note.current_family: Picidae (9th consecutive, all-time record, streak closes)
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Picidae count: 11 entries (Northern Flicker 21-22, Red-breasted Sapsucker 54, Pileated Woodpecker 54, Acorn 107, Pileated laughing 108, Pileated drumming 109, Hairy 110, Downy 111, Lewis's 112, White-headed 113, Three-toed 114, Black-backed 115)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Picoides from Latin picus ('woodpecker') + Greek -oides ('resembling') -- 'woodpecker-like.' arcticus from Latin arcticus ('of the north, arctic') -- referring to the species' boreal range. The Black-backed Woodpecker: the northern woodpecker-like bird.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Picoides shared with Three-toed Woodpecker (degree 114). The two three-toed North American woodpeckers. Consecutive Picoides entries (114-115).
taxonomy_note.order_note: Piciformes. Ninth consecutive (107-115). The longest Piciformes streak in the engine. Closes at degree 115.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Picidae streak closes at degree 115. Nine consecutive -- all-time record. Degree 116 introduces Cinclidae (dippers), a new family. The transition from fire to water.

song_metrics.fundamental_frequency_range_hz: 1,500-4,000 Hz. Rattle call -- sharp, staccato, percussive. Drum -- 500-2,000 Hz.
song_metrics.peak_energy_band_hz: 2,000-3,500 Hz. The sharp quality of the rattle carries across open post-fire landscape.
song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Rattle call: 1,500-4,000 Hz, rapid staccato burst. Drum: 500-2,000 Hz, fast dry roll on dead snags.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Rattle call: 1-3 seconds. A rapid burst of sharp percussive notes. Drum: 1-2 seconds, fast accelerating roll.
song_metrics.note_structure: RATTLE: Rapid series of sharp, hard notes -- sharper and louder than Three-toed (114). Percussive, insistent, functional. DRUM: Fast, slightly accelerating roll on charred dead snags. Higher-pitched and drier than Pileated's resonant boom.
song_metrics.note_types: Rattle call (primary, territorial/contact), drum (territorial/mate-attraction), soft pik contact notes. The rattle is the diagnostic vocalization.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Single burst. The rattle is one continuous staccato burst. The drum is one continuous roll. No repeated phrases within a single delivery.
song_metrics.timbre: Sharp. Percussive. Dry. The rattle has a hard, cutting quality. The drum is drier and higher-pitched than larger woodpeckers' drums. Both shaped by the charred-snag substrate and open post-fire acoustic environment.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- loud, assertive, carrying. Not the overwhelming E=6 of the Pileated, but a territorial sentinel's declaration across open burn landscape. The lack of canopy means sound travels unobstructed.
song_metrics.tempo: Rapid. Both rattle and drum are fast -- the rattle is a quick staccato burst, the drum slightly accelerates.
song_metrics.context: TERRITORIAL AND CONTACT. The rattle declares territory in post-fire landscape. The drum attracts mates and establishes boundaries. Given year-round but most frequently in breeding season.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Minimal. Rattle and drum are consistent across the species' range.
song_metrics.key_property: LOUD RATTLE CALL + UNIQUE DRUM -- the fire ghost's acoustic signature. E=5. Sharp staccato rattle carrying across open post-fire landscape. Dry percussive drum on charred snags. 9th CONSECUTIVE PICIDAE (107-115) -- ALL-TIME FAMILY RECORD surpassing Sciuridae octet. STREAK CLOSES. Second fire-specialist (after Lewis's 112). Three-toed (shared with 114). Obligate fire-follower. Conservation paradox.

plumage.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Entirely black back (no white barring or patterning). Black head with yellow crown patch (males). White underparts with black barring on flanks. The all-black back is diagnostic -- camouflage against charred bark.
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 115: (1) ALL-BLACK BACK -- no white barring, unique among PNW woodpeckers. Camouflage on charred bark. (2) YELLOW CROWN -- males have a bright yellow crown patch (not red like most woodpeckers). (3) THREE TOES -- two forward, one back. (4) BURN FOREST HABITAT -- found in recently burned areas with standing dead trees.
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Males have yellow crown; females lack it or have reduced yellow.
plumage.flight_pattern: Undulating flight typical of woodpeckers. The all-black back is visible in flight, distinguishing from other medium-sized woodpeckers.

physical_description.overall: Medium-sized woodpecker. Body length 23-25 cm. Wingspan 39-42 cm. Weight 61-88 g. Entirely black back. Black head with yellow crown (males). White underparts. Three toes.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 115: medium-sized woodpecker with entirely black back in recently burned forest. Yellow crown on males. Three toes visible at close range. If you see a black-backed woodpecker flaking bark off charred snags in a burn -- it is a Black-backed Woodpecker.
physical_description.field_id: All-black back (no white), yellow crown (males), three toes, in recently burned forest. The fire ghost. If the habitat is burned snags and the woodpecker's back is solid black -- Black-backed Woodpecker.

nodes.title: 9th Consecutive Picidae -- ALL-TIME FAMILY RECORD (107-115)
nodes.description: Nine consecutive Picidae entries. Surpasses the Sciuridae octet (8) as the all-time family record. The nonet: Acorn (107), Pileated laughing (108), Pileated drumming (109), Hairy (110), Downy (111), Lewis's (112), White-headed (113), Three-toed (114), Black-backed (115). Streak closes at 115.

nodes.title: The Fire Ghost -- Obligate Fire-Follower
nodes.description: Requires recently burned forest (1-8 years post-fire). Bark beetle specialist. All-black back as camouflage on charred bark. Conservation paradox: needs fire, but fire suppression reduces habitat. Salvage logging removes critical snag habitat.

nodes.title: The Fire Rattle -- Carrying Across the Burn
nodes.description: Loud sharp staccato rattle call + dry percussive drum on charred snags. Carries across open post-fire landscape (no canopy obstruction). E=5. Louder than Three-toed (114), harsher than Hairy (110), less wild than Pileated (108).

nodes.title: Consecutive Three-Toed Woodpeckers (114-115)
nodes.description: Three-toed (114) and Black-backed (115) -- the only two three-toed woodpeckers in North America. Both Picoides. Both bark-flakers. Three-toed foot provides wider swing arc, more force per strike. Consecutive entries documenting convergent adaptation.

nodes.title: Fire-Specialist Pair -- Lewis's (112) + Black-backed (115)
nodes.description: Two fire-specialists in the Picidae streak. Lewis's (112, flycatching, E=4, opportunistic fire-dependence). Black-backed (115, bark-flaking, E=5, obligate fire-dependence). Spectrum from opportunistic to obligate.

nodes.title: Picidae Streak Closes -- Fire to Water
nodes.description: Degree 115 closes the Picidae streak. Degree 116 introduces Cinclidae (dippers) -- a new family. The transition from burn forest to rushing stream. Fire to water. The engine's most dramatic ecosystem shift.

cross_references.relationship: CONSECUTIVE THREE-TOED WOODPECKERS. Both have three toes. Both are bark-flakers. Both are Picoides. Three-toed (114, boreal/burn, E=4); Black-backed (115, burn, E=5). Back-to-back. Energy escalation.
FIRE-SPECIALIST PAIR. Lewis's (112, flycatching, E=4, opportunistic). Black-backed (115, bark-flaking, E=5, obligate). Spectrum of fire-dependence. Two fire-specialists in the same streak.
PICIDAE ENERGY PEAK AND RANGE. Pileated at E=6 (maximum). Black-backed at E=5. The streak's energy range: E=3 to E=6 across nine degrees.
STREAK BOOKENDS. Acorn opens (107, colonial, oak savanna). Black-backed closes (115, solitary, burn forest). Community to solitude. Standing forest to charred landscape.

FIRE ECOLOGY CONTRAST. Spotted Owl needs old-growth standing forest alive. Black-backed needs standing forest dead and burned. Both need standing trees -- one alive, one dead. The fire management paradox.
